Fig. 1. Graphic diagram of the single (a) flared-U shaped spring beam and (b) proof or seismic mass.
(Labels include: SL₁, SL₂, SL₃, SL₄, SL₅, SL₆, SL₇, SL₈, SW₁, SW₂, SW₃, D₁, D₀, MW₁, MW₂, MW₃, ML₁, ML₂, ML₃.)

**Image Description:**
This technical graphic diagram illustrates two components: (a) a flared-U shaped spring beam and (b) a proof or seismic mass. The image functions as a dimensional chart detailing the geometry of these mechanical parts. Key objects include the U-shaped beam and the rectangular mass structure. Technical content is provided through numerous labeled dimensions (e.g., SL₁, SW₂, ML₃, MW₂), which define lengths, widths, and internal diameters (D₀, D₁). The diagram uses labels to specify precise measurements for structural analysis or manufacturing specifications.

**Extracted Text:**
Fig. 3. Graphic 3-D project of the flared-U shaped Device-1 comprising all the spring layers viz., Pt/Ti/PZT/Ti/Pt/SiO2/Si. Flared-U shaped springs, Bonding pad to top electrode, Bonding pad to bottom electrode, Supporting frame, Supporting spacer, Proof mass, Spring layers, Pt (0.15µm), Ti (0.05µm), PZT (2µm), Ti (0.05µm), Pt (0.15µm), SiO₂ (0.5µm), Si (5µm).

***

**Image Description:**
This figure is a 3D cross-section of a MEMS device, labeled as Device-1. The structure comprises a central **Proof mass** suspended by **flared-U shaped springs**. Key components include the supporting frame and spacer, along with bonding pads for top and bottom electrodes.

The technical content details the layered composition of the spring stack: Si (5µm) / SiO₂ (0.5µm) / Pt (0.15µm) / Ti (0.05µm) / PZT (2µm) / Ti (0.05µm) / Pt (0.15µm). The diagram includes standard Cartesian axes labels (x, y, z), indicating the device's physical dimensions and structure.

**Extracted Text:**

**Caption:**
Fig. 10. COMSOL simulated results of rms voltage and power output with the variation of frequency for flared-U shaped. (a) Device-1.1 (comprising PZT-4). (b) Device-1.2 (comprising PZT-5A). (c) Device-1.3 (comprising PZT-5H). (d) Device-1.4 (comprising PZT-8) at input acceleration of 0.07 g.

**Chart Labels/Axes:**
*   **X-axis:** Frequency (Hz)
*   **Left Y-axis:** Average power (nW)
*   **Right Y-axis:** Output rms voltage (mV)
*   **Legend:** Power, Voltage

***

**Image Description (Under 100 words):**

This figure presents four line charts (a-d), showing COMSOL simulated results of energy harvesting performance. The **technical content** compares the output power and rms voltage across varying frequencies for flared-U shaped devices using different piezoelectric materials (PZT-4, PZT-5A, PZT-5H, PZT-8).

The **charts** plot Average Power (nW) and Output rms Voltage (mV) against Frequency (Hz). All graphs exhibit a clear **graph trend**: both power and voltage peak sharply at a specific resonant frequency before decreasing rapidly. This demonstrates the optimal operating range for each material under 0.07 g acceleration.

Fig. 4. Manufacturing process flow of the proposed PVEH Device-1 comprising single proof mass and two beams of flared-U shaped springs.
(a) through (f)
Silicon, $\text{SiO}_2$, Pt/Ti electrodes, Piezo, Pt/Ti pads

***

**Image Description:**

This figure illustrates the manufacturing process flow for a proposed PVEH Device-1, which features a single proof mass and two flared-U shaped beams. The diagram is a technical representation detailing the sequential microfabrication steps (a) through (f). Key objects include layers of Silicon, $\text{SiO}_2$, Piezo material, and Pt/Ti electrodes/pads. This process flow demonstrates how various materials are deposited and patterned onto the substrate to construct the functional MEMS-like device structure. No charts or graphs are present.

**Extracted Text:**
Fig. 2. Schematic diagram of the flared-U shaped Device-1. $W_{SF}$, $L_A$, $L_D$, $W_A$, $W_D$, $SL_A$.

***

**Image Description (Short):**

This schematic diagram, titled "Fig. 2. Schematic diagram of the flared-U shaped Device-1," illustrates the geometry of a U-shaped device structure. It is purely technical content and does not contain charts or graphs. The image uses various labeled dimensions to define its physical parameters. Key labels include $W_{SF}$ (flaring width), $L_A$ and $L_D$ (overall lengths), $W_A$ and $W_D$ (widths), and $SL_A$. These measurements precisely map the structure, which features a central U-shaped element with flared ends.

**Extracted Text:**
Fig. 7. COMSOL simulated (a) stress distribution at 1 MPa boundary load and (b) relation of the stress versus the vertical dislocation of the proof mass for Device-1.3 with the variation of 1–5 g input acceleration.

Surface: von Mises stress (MPa).
(a), (b), mm, y, z, x, Stress (MPa), Mass displacement (mm).
Legend labels: For 1g, For 2g, For 3g, For 4g, For 5g.

***

**Image Description:**

Figure 7 presents COMSOL simulations for Device-1.3, detailing structural stress under varying loads. Panel (a) is a contour plot showing the von Mises stress distribution across the device surface. Panel (b) is a line chart plotting Stress (MPa) versus Mass displacement (mm). The graph exhibits a strong positive linear trend: as mass displacement increases, the stress rises proportionally for all tested input accelerations (1g to 5g). This technical content illustrates how boundary loads and acceleration affect the proof mass's structural integrity.

**Extracted Text:**

From Description: "Fig. 6. COMSOL simulated (a) 3-D view and (b) side view of the Device-1.3 proof mass tip displacement under no input vibration."
From Image Labels/Axes: "Liu et al. [22]", "From our simulation method based model", "Output rms voltage (mV)", "Frequency (Hz)", "Surface: Total displacement (mm)", "freq = 27.4 Hz".

***

**Image Description:**

This figure is a technical chart comparing the output rms voltage versus frequency for a device's resonance analysis. The main graph plots two models—Liu et al. [22] and the simulation model—showing a clear trend: a sharp peak in output voltage near 27.4 Hz, indicating resonance. Axes labels are "Output rms voltage (mV)" and "Frequency (Hz)". An inset 3D surface plot visualizes the total displacement of the proof mass at this frequency. This content relates to COMSOL simulations of Device-1.3's tip displacement under no input vibration.

**Extracted Text:**

Fig. 6. COMSOL simulated (a) 3-D view and (b) side view of the Device-1.3 proof mass tip displacement under no input vibration.

(Figure b axes labels: z, y, x; scale from -0.4 mm to 0.25 mm)

***

**Image Description:**

This figure presents COMSOL simulations of the Device-1.3 proof mass tip displacement under no input vibration. The image contains two contour plots showing "Total displacement (mm)." Figure (a) provides a 3D view, while Figure (b) offers a side cross-section. Both charts utilize spatial axes (x, y, z) and display the magnitude of displacement using color gradients. The technical content illustrates how the proof mass distributes its displacement across the surface. The trends show varying displacements ranging from negative values (e.g., -0.4 mm) to positive values (up to 0.25 mm), detailing the structural movement without external excitation.

***

**Image Description:**

This figure presents four 3D charts from a COMSOL simulation, illustrating the total displacement over natural frequency modes of a flared-U shaped device. The technical content compares results for different piezoelectric materials (PZT-4 through PZT-8). Each subplot displays the spatial distribution of displacement at specific eigenfrequencies (ranging from 28 to 30 Hz). Axes labels include spatial coordinates and a color bar representing Total displacement in millimeters. The charts show varying deformation patterns, demonstrating how material composition affects the structural response under different natural frequencies.

(a) Eigenfrequency=30.099 Hz, Surface: von Mises stress (MPa)

***

**Image Description:**
This figure presents four 3D charts illustrating COMSOL simulated stress distributions over natural frequency modes of a flared-U shaped device. The technical content analyzes the von Mises stress (in MPa) for different material compositions (PZT-4 through PZT-8). Each subplot shows a specific eigenfrequency and corresponding stress pattern. The plots demonstrate how varying the piezoelectric material affects the stress concentration across the structure, with axes labeling spatial dimensions in mm and color bars indicating stress magnitude.

**Extracted Text:**
Fig. 11. COMSOL simulated results of the output rms voltage and power with the variation of load resistance at fixed 1st resonant frequency and 0.07 g acceleration for flared-U shaped flared-U shaped. (a) Device-1.1 (comprising PZT-4). (b) Device-1.2 (comprising PZT-5A). (c) Device-1.3 (comprising PZT-5H). (d) Device-1.4 (comprising PZT-8).

**Image Description:**
This figure presents four line charts ((a)-(d)) showing COMSOL simulated results for energy harvesting devices using various PZT materials (PZT-4 to PZT-8). The graphs plot "Average power (nW)" and "Output rms voltage (mV)" against varying "Load resistance ($\text{k}\Omega$)". All panels demonstrate a clear trend: both output power and voltage initially increase as load resistance increases, reaching an optimal peak before stabilizing or slightly decreasing. This illustrates the performance variation of flared-U shaped devices under specific acceleration conditions.

IEEE TRANSACTIONS ON INDUSTRY APPLICATIONS, VOL. 58, NO. 3, MAY/JUNE 2022
3901
A Comparative Simulation Study of the Different
Variations of PZT Piezoelectric Material by Using A
MEMS Vibration Energy Harvester
Bapi Debnath
and R. Kumar
Abstract—In this article, the presented work aims to scavenge
maximum electrical outputs by using the novel microelectrome-
chanical system ﬂared-U shaped spring based piezoelectric vi-
bration energy harvester (PVEH). The performance studies were

bration energy harvester (PVEH). The performance studies were
carried out by employing four different variations of lead zirconate
titanate (PZT) piezoelectric material namely PZT-4, PZT-5A, PZT-
5H, and PZT-8 in the same size and shape of the proposed device.
The maximum output powers employing PZT-4, PZT-5A, PZT-
5H, and PZT-8 piezoelectric materials are 7.156 nW, 8.657 nW,
10.738 nW, 5.701 nW over 30 Hz, 28.4 Hz, 28.6 Hz, and 30.2 Hz,

10.738 nW, 5.701 nW over 30 Hz, 28.4 Hz, 28.6 Hz, and 30.2 Hz,
resonant frequency, respectively, at input acceleration of 0.07 g.
From the ﬁnite element method simulator COMSOL Multiphysics
5.4 (licensed version) simulation-based outcomes, it is inferred
that PZT-5H piezoelectric material performs better than other
variations of PZT, such as PZT-4, PZT-5A, and PZT-8 piezoelectric
materials.
Index Terms—COMSOL, energy harvester, ﬁnite element

materials.
Index Terms—COMSOL, energy harvester, ﬁnite element
method (FEM), ﬂared-U shaped, microelectromechanical system
(MEMS), piezoelectric, lead zirconate titanate (PZT), resonant
frequency.
I. INTRODUCTION
R
ECENTLY, researchers have shown a huge interest in
microscale ambient energy harvesting devices used for
delivering power to the autonomous microsystems, by taking the
advantage of less power consumable sensors, microprocessors,

advantage of less power consumable sensors, microprocessors,
and wireless sensor network nodes. The main idea behind this
is to replace the used battery in microsystems because a battery
needs to be charged or replaced after some time. The vibration
energy is a very suitable form of energy for power scavenging
because of its widespread existence in our surroundings, but the
survey indicates that most of the surrounding vibration sources

survey indicates that most of the surrounding vibration sources
have low frequency (≤200 Hz) and acceleration (≤0.5 g) peak
[1]. There are several techniques for ambient vibration power
scavenging [2], but the piezoelectric scavenging method is most

considerable because a piezoelectric vibration energy harvester

considerable because a piezoelectric vibration energy harvester
(PVEH) can generate high power density and able to produce
enough electric energy in a limited area [3], [4].
However, a lot of microelectromechanical system (MEMS)-
based research works had been done on high frequency and
acceleration-based vibration power scavenging. He et al. [5]
demonstrated a cantilever-based PVEH that generates 56.4 μW
(power) at 1 g (acceleration) and 210.85 Hz (frequency). Minh

(power) at 1 g (acceleration) and 210.85 Hz (frequency). Minh
et al. [6] presented a PVEH that generates 731 nW at 1509 Hz.
Kanno et al. [7] reported the maximum obtained average power
outputs from two PVEHs having two different piezoelectric
materials such as lead zirconate titanate (PZT) and potassium
sodium niobate (KNN), which were 1.0 and 1.1 μW at 892 and
1036 Hz frequencies, respectively. Isarakorn et al. [8] fabricated
aPZTpiezoelectricmaterial-basedcantilevertypePVEHhaving

aPZTpiezoelectricmaterial-basedcantilevertypePVEHhaving
13 μW·g−2 power output at 2.3 kHz (resonant frequency). Lei
et al. [9] presented a unimorph silicon/PZT based PVEH that
generates 14.0 μW at 1 g and 235 Hz. Hirasawa et al. [10]
demonstrated a PVEH comprising AlN piezoelectric material
based corrugated beam structure for 0.18 μW power harvesting
at 853 Hz and 1 g. Park et al. [11] reported a PVEH employing
an interdigital shaped electrode for 1.1 μW power scavenging

an interdigital shaped electrode for 1.1 μW power scavenging
at 0.39 g and 528 Hz. These statistics inspired the specialists to
develop new low-frequency and acceleration utilizable PVEH.
Meanwhile, several researchers worked on low-frequency-based
PVEHs [12]–[15].
In this article, the intention is to analyze the electrical response
of four different variations of PZT piezoelectric material (such as
PZT-4, PZT-5A, PZT-5H, and PZT-8) by using a novel MEMS

PZT-4, PZT-5A, PZT-5H, and PZT-8) by using a novel MEMS
ﬂared-U shaped spring-based PVEH, which is speculated by
Debnath and Kumar [16] comprising only PZT-5H. To evaluate
the device performances, the simulation study is carried out by
using ﬁnite element method (FEM)-based simulator COMSOL
Multiphysics (licensed version). Using the same size and di-
mensions of the proposed ﬂared-U shaped spring-based PVEH,
four different devices utilize four different variations of PZT to

four different devices utilize four different variations of PZT to
harvest the low vibration energy with enhanced power conver-
sion capability. Finally, a comparison is made among proposed
ﬂared-U shaped devices and other reported PVEHs.
II. DYNAMIC MODELING AND DEVICE CONFIGURATION
A. Energy Harvesting System Modeling
Since the MEMS-based PVEHs are excited by external dis-
placement or motion of their frame (also known as base), the

placement or motion of their frame (also known as base), the

B. Device Conﬁguration

y-applied stress direction, respectively.
B. Device Conﬁguration
For low-frequency-based energy scavenging, two compos-
ite piezoelectric cantilever based ﬂared-U shaped springs with
central mass (comprising an area of 6 mm2 and a thickness of
0.300 mm) are designed. The proposed schematic construction
of the ﬂared-U shaped spring and proof or seismic mass are
illustrated in Fig. 1, and corresponding in-detail dimensions are
shown in Tables I and II, respectively. Fig. 2 and Table III delin-

shown in Tables I and II, respectively. Fig. 2 and Table III delin-
eate schematic drawing and dimensions of the proposed ﬂared-U
shaped harvester Device-1, respectively. The three-dimensional
(3-D) layout of the whole ﬂared-U shaped harvester Device-1
including all layers in the springs is demonstrated in Fig. 3.
Several PVEH devices based on U-shaped structures have been
reported before such device design was used by Heinrich et al.

reported before such device design was used by Heinrich et al.
[17] and Su and Shih [18]. However, these designs are different
from our proposed PVEH Device-1. The arrangement of two
symmetrical ﬂared-U shape springs and one proof mass is a
novel PVEH design for vibration power scavenging. To achieve
a high-power density based PVEH, four different variations of
PZT piezoelectric material, such as PZT-4, PZT-5A, PZT-5H,
and PZT-8, have been considered for this investigation. Even

and PZT-8, have been considered for this investigation. Even
though corresponding four devices, such as Device-1.1, 1.2, 1.3,
and 1.4, consist of four different PZT piezoelectric materials but
they entail the same dimensions and size of the proposed ﬂared-
U shaped springs-based Device-1 design. The manufacturing
process ﬂow of the proposed Device-1, based on six fabrication
steps, is illustrated in Fig. 4.

III. FEM SIMULATION
For the sake of assessing the ﬁnest device design and shape,
FEM simulations were done in COMSOL Multiphysics 5.4

FEM simulations were done in COMSOL Multiphysics 5.4
(licensed version) by using a FEM model based PVEH system.
An FEM model of the energy harvester consists of ﬂared-U
shaped piezoelectric unimorph spring structures mounted at
one end with a seismic mass and clamped on the other end
with frame. Before starting simulations in COMSOL to inves-
tigate the ﬂared-U shaped springs-based device, the mesh was
composed of ﬁve types of elements, such as prism elements

composed of ﬁve types of elements, such as prism elements
(18 158), triangular elements (20 640), quadrilateral elements
(4024), edge elements (4810), and vertex elements (260), for
a total number of 272 340 degrees of freedom for the rest
of simulations. “Eigenfrequency” analysis in COMSOL was
considered for the system to obtain the natural frequencies and
relative mode shapes. “Eigenfrequency” analysis in COMSOL
Fig. 4.
Manufacturing process ﬂow of the proposed PVEH Device-1 compris-

Fig. 4.
Manufacturing process ﬂow of the proposed PVEH Device-1 compris-
ing single proof mass and two beams of ﬂared-U shaped springs.
was also done to obtain relative stress concentrations over the
natural frequencies. “Stationary” analysis in COMSOL was
done to observe the static failure limits of the proposed device.
Since the device should be mounted to a piece of vibrating host
it is analyzed by acceleration characterized as a base excitation

it is analyzed by acceleration characterized as a base excitation
during “Frequency Domain” study in COMSOL. Using COM-
SOL, the combination of “Solid Mechanics,” “Electrostatics,”
and “Electrical Circuit” Physics, were used to evaluate the
mechanical and the electrical behavior of the proposed design.
During the power and voltage output analysis in COMSOL, a
sinusoidal vertical acceleration was applied to the device and
the body load or weight due to gravity was given as input to

the body load or weight due to gravity was given as input to
the piezoelectric layer to generate strain. The bottom and top
electrodes of the device were connected with an electric circuit
element in COMSOL to deﬁne the resistor value. In “Frequency
Domain” analysis, frequency was kept constant to analyze the
voltage and power output with variations of load resistance. The
material properties and coefﬁcients of the piezoelectric materials

material properties and coefﬁcients of the piezoelectric materials
used for the simulations and analysis of the proposed design
were aggregated from the published data [19]–[22] and shown
in Tables IV and V, respectively.
For our simulation method validation purpose, we compared
our simulated method-based results with previously published

that simulated and experimented PVEH results follow a good

is applicable for an actual system.
IV. RESULTS AND DISCUSSION
The active area and volume of the ﬂared-U shaped harvester
Device-1 are 10.141 mm2 and 1.8861 mm3 , respectively. Due
to the gravity, proof mass of the proposed PVEH device will
encounter certain displacement. Fig. 6(a) demonstrates FEM
simulated mass tip deformation of the ﬂared-U shaped Device-

simulated mass tip deformation of the ﬂared-U shaped Device-
1.3 under the condition of no vibration and acceleration input.
The distance covered by the proof mass due to gravitational
force (9.807 m/s2) is obtained to be 0.29576 mm (Md), as
demonstrated in Fig. 6(b). To investigate stress concentration
around the ﬂared-U shaped springs having 0.1 mm inner curve
diameter (DI) and 0.3 mm outer curve diameter (DO) at corners,
FEM simulator COMSOL is used to deﬁne 1 MPa boundary

FEM simulator COMSOL is used to deﬁne 1 MPa boundary
load as force per unit area on the top surface of the Device-1.3.

V. CONCLUSION

V. CONCLUSION
In conclusion, we proposed a novel MEMS-based PVEH con-
sisting of two symmetrical ﬂared-U shaped composite springs
and a bulk silicon mass. Four different variations of PZT
piezoelectric materials, such as PZT-4, PZT-5A, PZT-5H, and
PZT-8, have been considered for this investigation to achieve
the high-power density based PVEH. Device-1.1 to Device-

the high-power density based PVEH. Device-1.1 to Device-
1.4 consist of four different piezoelectric materials but they
entail the same dimensions and size of the proposed ﬂared-U
Authorized licensed use limited to: ANNA UNIVERSITY. Downloaded on May 26,2026 at 03:51:40 UTC from IEEE Xplore.  Restrictions apply.

3908
IEEE TRANSACTIONS ON INDUSTRY APPLICATIONS, VOL. 58, NO. 3, MAY/JUNE 2022
shaped Device-1 design. The COMSOL simulation based test-
ing outcomes demonstrated that these devices can be applied
for scavenging energy from environments with low vibrations.
The simulation results of the devices show that Device-1.3
PVEH entailing PZT-5H piezoelectric material exhibits max-
imum normalized areal output power density than the other
three devices (Device-1.1, Device-1.2, and Device-1.4), which

three devices (Device-1.1, Device-1.2, and Device-1.4), which
have the same active area and volume as Device-1.3 but contain
different piezoelectric materials. The simulation outcomes of
the PVEH devices demonstrate that the voltage increases with
the rise of load resistance but the power increases ﬁrst and, after
sometime, it starts decreasing with the rise of load resistance.
After exciting the ﬂared-U shaped springs-based Device-1.3

After exciting the ﬂared-U shaped springs-based Device-1.3
(comprising PZT-5H) at 0.07 g, the maximum power and voltage
output of 10.738 nW and 50.76 mV, respectively, were obtained
over resonant frequency of 28.6 Hz and optimum load resistance
of 120 kΩ.

ical systems (MEMS) based piezoelectric vibration
energy harvester (PVEH) from the National Institute
of Technology Nagaland, Dimapur, India, in 2021.
His research interests include MEMS and MEMS-
